steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: the first ends with "Laffer [blank]" and the second begins with "a theoretical relationship between tax rates and revenues." The blank must provide punctuation that correctly joins two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — no punctuation (“curve”): fuses two independent clauses with no separator, creating a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — semicolon (“curve;”): a semicolon correctly joins two independent clauses. The second clause then uses “a theoretical relationship between tax rates and revenues” as an appositive for “the curve,” set off by commas — grammatically sound. ✓
  • Choice C — comma + relative clause (“curve, which is”): “which is” turns “a theoretical relationship between tax rates and revenues” into a relative clause modifying “curve,” but then “the curve was famously sketched…” is left as an independent clause with no connector — producing a comma splice or fragment. ✗
  • Choice D — comma alone (“curve,”): joining two independent clauses with only a comma is a comma splice. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
